coredata.dc:description: Inflenza A viruses (IAVs) are highly transmissible and pathogenic Orthomyxoviruses, which have led to worldwide outbreaks and seasonal pandemics of acute respiratory diseases, causing serious threats to public health. Currently used anti-influenza drugs may cause neurological side effects, and they are increasingly less effective against mutant strains. To help prevent the spread of IAVs, in this work, we have developed quercetin-derived carbonized nanogels (CNGsQur) that display potent viral inhibitory, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. The antiviral CNGsQur were synthesized by mild carbonization of quercetin (Qur), which successfully preserved their antioxidative and anti-inflammatory properties while also contributed enhanced properties, such as water solubility, viral binding, and biocompatibility. Antiviral assays of co-treatment, pre-treatment, and post-treatment indicate that CNGsQur interacts with the virion, revealing that the major antiviral mechanism resulting in the inhibition of the virus is by their attachment on the cell surface. Among them, the selectivity index (SI) of CNGsQur270 (>857.1) clearly indicated its great potential for clinical application in IAVs inhibition, which was much higher than that of pristine quercetin (63.7) and other clinical drugs (4–81). Compared with quercetin at the same dose, the combined effects of viral inhibition, antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities impart the superior therapeutic effects of CNGsQur270 aerosol inhalation in the treatment of IAVs infection, as evidenced by a mouse model. These CNGsQur effectively prevent the spread of IAVs and suppress virus-induced inflammation while also exhibiting good in vivo biocompatibility. CNGsQur shows much promise as a clinical therapeutic agent against infection by IVAs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of purified CNGsQur. (B) The proposed formation mechanism of CNGsQur. The red arrows indicate the in situ formation of carbonized dots. (C) UV–vis absorption and (D) fluorescence spectra of quercetin and CNGsQur. Insets to (C) and (D) are the photographs of the corresponding solutions under white light and excitation under UV lamp (365 nm), respectively. Fluorescence spectra of CNGs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Data are the mean ± SD of three separate experiments.The concentration of all the solutions was 0.2 mg mL−1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Plaque reduction rate (%) of quercetin and CNGsQur against H1N1 infection by (a) co-treatment, (b) pre-treatment, and (c) post-treatment viral plaque assays. Data are the mean ± SD of three separate experiments. (B) TEM images of (a) H1N1 virus, (b) CNGsQur270, and (c) H1N1 virus (1 × 106 PFU mL−1) incubated with CNGsQur270 (10 μg mL−1) for 10 min. The red arrows indicate the location of H1N1 virus. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Antioxidant activities of quercetin and CNGsQur that estimated by using (A) DPPH assay and (B) Folin-Ciocalteu assay. The concentration of test solutions were 0.02 mg mL−1. (C) Effects of quercetin and CNGsQur270 on the LPS-induced NO expression levels in RAW 264.7 cells. RAW 264.7 cells were treated with quercetin and CNGsQur270 cultured with LPS (1.0 μg mL−1) for 20 h. The positive control was RAW 264.7 cells treated with LPS only. The NO production was determined by the Greiss reagent. Data are the mean ± SD of three separate experiments.

(A) Survival rate, (B) clinical score, and (C) original weight of BALB/c mice after H1N1 infection and then quercetin or CNGsQur270 treatment. The mice were infected through aerosol inhalation with H1N1 virus (10 LD50). Quercetin or CNGsQur270 (500 μg mL−1, 5 mL) were given through aerosol inhalation until day 7. Data are the mean ± SD of 12 mice for each group.

Histopathology of the mice infected with H1N1 virus and then 7-days without or with aerosol inhalation with quercetin or CNGsQur270. Lung tissues were collected and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) to assess the general histopathology. Black arrow indicates alveolar septal thickening; red arrows indicate microvascular thrombosis; green arrows indicate the immune cells. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematic representation of (A) one-step synthesis of carbonized nanogels (CNGs) from quercetin and (B) their application for the treatment of H1N1 virus through blocking of the viral attachment to lung epithelial cells and alleviating oxidative stress and inflammation.
Cytotoxicity (CC50), antiviral activities (EC50), and selectivity index (SI) values of quercetin and CNGsQur against H1N1 virus in MDCK cells by co-treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflenza A viruses (IAVs) are highly transmissible and pathogenic Orthomyxoviruses, which have led to worldwide outbreaks and seasonal pandemics of acute respiratory diseases, causing serious threats to public health. Currently used anti-influenza drugs may cause neurological side effects, and they are increasingly less effective against mutant strains. To help prevent the spread of IAVs, in this work, we have developed quercetin-derived carbonized nanogels (CNGsQur) that display potent viral inhibitory, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. The antiviral CNGsQur were synthesized by mild carbonization of quercetin (Qur), which successfully preserved their antioxidative and anti-inflammatory properties while also contributed enhanced properties, such as water solubility, viral binding, and biocompatibility. Antiviral assays of co-treatment, pre-treatment, and post-treatment indicate that CNGsQur interacts with the virion, revealing that the major antiviral mechanism resulting in the inhibition of the virus is by their attachment on the cell surface. Among them, the selectivity index (SI) of CNGsQur270 (>857.1) clearly indicated its great potential for clinical application in IAVs inhibition, which was much higher than that of pristine quercetin (63.7) and other clinical drugs (4–81). Compared with quercetin at the same dose, the combined effects of viral inhibition, antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities impart the superior therapeutic effects of CNGsQur270 aerosol inhalation in the treatment of IAVs infection, as evidenced by a mouse model. These CNGsQur effectively prevent the spread of IAVs and suppress virus-induced inflammation while also exhibiting good in vivo biocompatibility. CNGsQur shows much promise as a clinical therapeutic agent against infection by IVAs.

ce:section.ce:para: Influenza is a seasonal respiratory illness and usually peaks in winter with an annual attack rate estimated at 20%–30% in children and 5%–10% in adults, causing over 5 million cases of severe illness and 250,000 deaths [1]. Influenza causes annual economic cost of over $11 billion in the United States [1]. Of the four types (A, B, C and D) of seasonal influenza viruses, only A and B types circulate and cause seasonal epidemics [2]. Influenza A viruses (IAVs) belong to the Orthomyxoviridae family. The IAV genome is comprised of eight segmented pieces of negative-sense single-stranded RNA [3,4]. Acute respiratory diseases caused by IAVs have incredible transmissibility, thus seriously impacting public health, society, and the economy [5]. Swine influenza lineages of IAVs continue to circulate in humans, such as the subtypes H1N1 and H3N2, causing great concern due to their pandemic potential and past outbreaks [6].

To initiate infection, IAVs rely on the protein hemagglutinin (HA) located on the virus envelope [7]. This protein enables the virus to bind to the host cell via sialic acids present on the cell surface, and it promotes endocytosis, allowing the viral particles to be transported and internalized in the endosome [8]. Once infected by IAVs, the defense mechanism of the host cells is activated. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated to increase cellular oxidative stress to trigger an immune response [9]. Excessive generation of ROS not only causes damage to the cells but also promotes the release of inflammatory cytokines and chemokines [10]. In addition, IAV infections elevate oxidative stress, and inflammation increases the permeability of blood vessels, resulting in airway mucosa, lung tissue edema, and multiple hemorrhages in the respiratory organs [11]. A combination of antioxidative molecules (e.g., N-acetyl-l-cysteine, vitamins C and E) and antiviral drugs (e.g., peramivir, ribavirin, oseltamivir, and zanamivir) can synergistically reduce cell damage and the lethal effects of influenza virus infection [12,13].

The rapid spread of IAVs caused by genetic variation limits the effectiveness of existing vaccines. This necessitates huge annual expenditures for vaccine updates [14]. Another tool against these viruses is anti-influenza drugs, such as amantadine, oseltamivir phosphate, peramivir, and baloxavir marboxil. Although these clinical drugs have potent antiviral capabilities in limiting viral replication, targeting M2 ion channels, neuraminidase, or cap-dependent endonucleases, they are accompanied by neurological side effects such as insomnia, nervousness, confusion, and abnormal behaviors, especially in children and adolescents [15–17]. Unfortunately, due to the rapid and ongoing genetic variation, drug-resistant viral strains are emerging around the world [18]. Consequentially, the development of novel strategies to prevent the spread of IAVs remains an urgent need.

Inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) can inhibit various viruses by releasing metal ions, generating ROS, or by causing structural damage to viral particles. NPs displaying these properties have been made from gold, silver, titanium dioxide, silicon dioxide, zinc oxide, and copper iodide [19–22]. The high cytotoxicity of metal NPs, however, limits their potential for clinical application. For example, intranasal delivery of metal and metal oxide NPs have been found to cause excessive production of ROS in basal cells, excessive mucus secretion by goblet cells, abnormal function of ciliated cells, and chronic inflammation of alveolar cells, thereby resulting in pulmonary fibrosis [23,24]. In sharp contrast, the antiviral efficacy of many carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), have been verified by experimental animal models while also displaying high biocompatibility and low side effects [25–32]. In other studies, carbon-based nanomaterials with highly antioxidative properties have also been reported, whereby they can remove ROS in living cells and reduce inflammation without harming the normal physiology [33–38]. The antiviral, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory properties of these carbon nanomaterials, however, have not yet been demonstrated in a single modality.

Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK, ATCC, CCL-34TM) cells and mouse mononuclear macrophages (RAW 264.7, ATCC, TIB-71-TM) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection. Analytical grade quercetin powder (≥ 95%) used in this study was purchased from Cayman Chemical. (Ann Arbor, MI, USA). The other general chemicals, including quercetin, sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium nitrate (Na2CO3), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), monosodium phosphate (NaH₂PO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), PrestoBlue, Griess reagent, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), ethanol, xylene, Folin-Ciocalteu reagent, and crystal violet were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA) and were of the highest purity available (> 96%). 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, HPLC grade) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). All culture media and chemicals related to cell culture were purchased from Gibco BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; 10 mM Na2HPO4, 140 mM NaCl, 2.70 mM KCl; adjusted to pH 7.4 using HCl) was used to mimic physiological conditions. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm; EMD Millipore, MA, USA) was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbonized nanogels from quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin carbonized nanogels (CNGsQur) were prepared based on our previous report with a slight modification of the procedure [41]. First, 50 mg of yellow powdered quercetin was placed in a glass scintillation vial and then pyrolyzed in a muffle furnace (DH 300, Dengyng, Taipei, Taiwan) in the air atmosphere at 230, 250, 270, and 290 °C for 2 h to form dark green, gray, dark gray, and black residues, respectively. These solid products were dissolved at room temperature with 5 mL of sodium phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 12) and sonicated (200 W; DC200H, Honeywell, Charlotte, NC, USA) for 2 h, and then allowed to stand overnight. Subsequently, we centrifuged the solutions with a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 5,000 g for 30 min at 25 °C. The brown supernatants were first dialyzed against deionized (DI) water for 5 h through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0.5–1.0 kDa; Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, CA, USA), with DI water changed every hour. Finally, the samples were further dialyzed against DI water for 18 h. The purified CNGsQur with the purity of > 97.5% were stored at 4 °C.

A monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Synergy 4 Multi-Mode; Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) was used to measure the fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of various CNGsQur. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated using quinine as the standard reference (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4). The morphology of the various CNGsQur was analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). The CNGsQur were diluted with sodium phosphate solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) to the appropriate concentration (50 μg mL−1) and deposited on 300-mesh carbon-coated copper grids at room temperature.

To prepare a virus stock, 1 × 105 Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells were seeded into 15-cm culture dishes and cultured at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator with Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM). After 24 h, influenza AH1N1HA1 (A/WSN/33)-infected MDCK cells were grown in DMEM supplemented with 2.0% fetal bovine serum (FBS) for 2–3 days until 80–90% of cells were lysed. Subsequently, the viral supernatant was isolated by centrifugation (750 g, 4 °C, 20 min). The virus stock solution was stored at −80 °C.

The virus titer was determined by a plaque assay. MDCK cells were seeded into six-well plates at a concentration of 1 × 105 cells well−1 with DMEM and incubated (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 12 h. After washing with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), the confluent monolayer MDCK cells were infected with 100 μL well−1 ten-fold serial dilutions of viral stock in DMEM for 1 h. The unadhered virions were removed and washed thrice with PBS, then overlaid with 2.0 mL of DMEM supplemented with 2.0% FBS and 0.3% agarose. After incubation for 48 h, the cells were fixed with 10% formalin for an hour and stained with 1.0% crystal violet. The plaque-forming units per mL (PFUs mL−1) were counted by visual examination.

Prior to the inspection, MDCK cells (1 × 105 cells well−1) were seeded into six-well plates to form confluent monolayer cells at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere overnight. To study the possible mechanism of quercetin and CNGsQur affecting the infectivity of influenza A virus H1N1 strain (H1N1), we have examined three different approaches: (i) co-treatment: MDCK cells were treated simultaneously with H1N1 (50 PFU well−1) and the tested substance (quercetin or CNGsQur) at different concentrations (1, 5, or 10 μg mL−1) for 1 h; (ii) pre-treatment: quercetin or CNGsQur were pre-treated with the MDCK cells for 1 h, then cultured with H1N1 for 1 h; and (iii) post-treatment: MDCK cells were pre-infected with H1N1 for 1 h, and then incubated with quercetin or CNGsQur for 1 h after virus removal. Negative (MDCK cells only) and positive (MDCK cells infected with H1N1 without tested materials) controls were also studied. The virus infection efficiency was measured using plaque assays. Inhibition rate of virus plaque formation (%) = [1− (number of virus plaques in the test group/number of virus plaques in the positive control group)] × 100%.

2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging activity and total phenolic contents (TPC) were determined to evaluate the antioxidative capacity of different CNGsQur. The operating protocol of the DPPH radical scavenging activity measurement was based on a previous report [42]. The reaction was performed by mixing 100 μM DPPH with an equal volume of test sample in a total volume of 300 μL. After 30 min of reaction in a dark box, the DPPH radical scavenging level is defined by measuring the absorption at 517 nm with Synergy 4 Multi-Mode microplate spectrophotometer.

The TPC of quercetin and CNGsQur was determined based on the Folin-Ciocalteu method reported earlier [43]. In a typical process, the test sample was mixed with Folin-Ciocalteu reagent in equal volume in a vial (total volume 200 μL). After the liquid mixture was reacted at room temperature for 30 min, 200 μL 10.0% Na2CO3 was added. The liquid mixtures were allowed to stand at room temperature for 30 min before the absorption at 760 nm was measured using the Synergy 4 Multi-Mode microplate spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM measurement of H1N1 bound to CNGsQur
ce:section.ce:para: Negative staining was applied to the samples containing viruses and CNGsQur. Before adding 4.0% paraformaldehyde, the H1N1 solution (1 × 106 PFU mL−1) and CNGsQur solution (20.0 μg mL−1) were mixed in equal volumes at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was deposited on a copper mesh, washed twice with DI water, and then stained with 2.0% phosphotungstic acid for 1 min. Subsequently, the excess liquid was absorbed using filter paper and vacuum dried for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiviral efficacy of CNGsQur270 in experimental BALB/c mice model
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the animal study was reviewed and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee, College of Life Sciences, National Taiwan Ocean University (IACUC Approval No. 108055). Six-week-old male BALB/c mice were purchased from the National Laboratory Animal Center (Taipei, Taiwan) and raised in the laboratory animal center of National Taiwan Ocean University (Keelung, Taiwan). The experiments were performed in an animal biosafety level 2 facility. During the period, mice challenged by the H1N1 were transported to a quarantine facility, with 12 mice for each group.

Experimental H1N1-infected mice were induced by spraying aerosol at a dose equivalent to 10 median lethal dose (10 LD50; ∼4 × 104 PFU H1N1) with aerosol particle diameters of 4–6 μm in a 4 L closed chamber using a nebulizer (Bio-Cando, Taipei, Taiwan). After 30 min, the mice that had naturally inhaled the aerosol containing virus were returned to the normal incubation chamber. After the virus challenge for 6 h, each infected mouse in the closed chamber was further sprayed aerosol sample from 8 mL PBS, quercetin (500 μg mL−1), or CNGsQur270 (500 μg mL−1). Twelve mice for each treated group. All mice were given the tested materials daily through aerosol inhalation until day 7. Important parameters, such as survival rate, clinical score, and body weight, were recorded daily for 14 days. The healthy score was employed to grade symptoms, with 10 representing healthy mice, 8 representing weight loss, ruffled fur or lethargy, 6 representing hunched and mild tachypnea, 4 representing severe tachypnea, labored breathing, greasy fur, and reduced mobility, and 0 representing death.

On day 7 of the experiment, 6 randomly selected mice were anesthetized with isoflurane, euthanized with CO2 gas, and then their lung tissues were harvested for further investigation with the histological examination and determination of inflammation indicators.

The results of the experimental data are represented as the standard deviation (SD) of the mean (mean ± SD) of three or four separate experiments, and the statistical analysis was carried out by Student's t-test, where p < 0.05, represents a statistically significant difference.

See the Supporting Information for the details on the comprehensive characteristics of various CNGsQur, NO level measurement, cytotoxic assay, hemolysis assay, RNA extraction and quantitative PCR, in vivo biocompatibility assessment, and histological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal-driven formation of supramolecular nanogels from quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: We prepared a series of CNGsQur by mild pyrolysis of quercetin powder in a pre-heated muffle furnace at 230, 250, 270, and 290 °C for 2 h; the obtained CNGsQur are denoted as CNGsQur230, CNGsQur250, CNGsQur270, and CNGsQur290, respectively. The yields of the purified CNGsQur were determined to be >78.1%, except for CNGsQur290 (32.5%) due to excessive carbonization at the higher temperature (Table S1, Supplementary Information). Note that the aqueous solubilities of all purified CNGsQur (>50 g L−1) were higher than that of pristine quercetin (3.0 × 10−2 g L−1). The low solubility of quercetin in aqueous solution is mainly due to being prone to uncontrolled self-assembly through hydrogen bonding and π − π stacking [44]. We speculated the surface functional groups of the CNGs inherited from the A-ring of quercetin react with the C-ring and undergo condensation during the pyrolysis and/or dissolution in alkaline solution (pH 12) [45]. This reaction leads to dense pyrolyzed quercetin products (i.e., quercetin's moieties) self-anchored on the CNGs, enhanced dispersibility, and a boost in their bioactivity.

The partially carbonized nanogel structures with irregular shapes, sizes ranging from 200 − 500 nm (Fig. 1 A), and display colloidal characteristics in an aqueous medium. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis shows that the hydrodynamic diameters of the CNGsQur230, CNGsQur250, CNGsQur270, and CNGsQur290 were ca. 225, 257, 375, and 341 nm, respectively (Table S1, Supplementary Information ). Compared to previously reported carbon dots, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), CQDs, and CPDs [46–49], our CNGs possess polymer–nanogel/graphene dot-like hybrid structures that are composed of interpenetrated networks of polymeric quercetin embedded with few-layered oxygen-dopped GQDs. It has been speculated that the formation of CNGs in the initial stage occurs through a condensation reaction to form oligomers or polymers (Fig. 1B). These further undergo crosslinking reactions to form a large gel-like structure (i.e., supramolecular structures) followed by carbonization. Carbonization, in turn, causes the fragmentation of the large supramolecular structures into smaller and irregular polymer framework nanostructures and in situ formation of the partially carbonized graphene dots.

We further examined the surface functional groups of the CNGsQur by various spectroscopic techniques. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CNGsQur exhibit an absorption band at 270 nm that we attribute to the π → π* transitions of graphene structures and conjugated CC bonds in the benzoyl system (formed by the A-C ring), and a shoulder band at 300–450 nm for the n → π* transitions of CO bonds in the cinnamoyl system (formed by the B-C ring) (Fig. 1C) [51]. Furthermore, the redshift of the absorption band with the synthesis temperature indicates that these chemical groups inherited from quercetin might undergo polymerization during the mild pyrolysis process to form a conjugated aromatic structure [52]. Compared to the other CNGsQur, CNGsQur270 display a strong signal in the shoulder band, suggesting that its surface might inherit functional groups ascribed to the cinnamoyl system of quercetin (Fig. 1C, yellow line). In terms of fluorescence, all CNGsQur exhibit a broad emission band around 460 nm under excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 1D). These fluorescent properties of the CNGs are mainly due to the π-conjugated macrocycle structure and the edge chemical functional groups [53,54]. In addition, the covalent bond crosslinking and supramolecular self-assembly may boost the emission of subfluorophores in the CNGs [53].The crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) is attributed to the decreased vibration and rotation of the subfluorophores in crosslinked gel structures [55]. Therefore, CNGsQur230 display the best fluorescence emission efficiency among the CNGsQur can ascribe to the highest rigid entanglement effect [53]. The fluorescence QYs of all CNGsQur are<1%, however, mainly because of a lack of nitrogen doping [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities of CNGsQur
ce:section.ce:para: The free radical scavenging ability of CNGsQur was assessed using the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay and compared to that of quercetin. Except for CNGsQur290, which was slightly lower, quercetin and all CNGsQur displayed the same level of radical scavenging ability (Fig. 3 A). The radical scavenging properties of CNGsQur are mainly due to their phenolic groups that are preserved even after carbonization. This process also forms conjugated CC bonds that further contribute to radical scavenging [68]. Even so, the slight decrease in the activity of CNGsQur290 may be due to over carbonization. We also used the Folin-Ciocalteu assay to evaluate the reducing activity of quercetin and the CNGsQur. Again, CNGsQur (except for CNGsQur290) display superior reducing ability (Fig. 3B). The highly antioxidative properties of the CNGsQur may be conferred by the incomplete carbonized structure formed under the relatively mild heating process; that is, the active polyphenolic and catechol functional groups derived from quercetin are polymerized and concentrated on the surface of CNGs. In particular, the chemically active hydroxyl/phenol groups on the B-C rings of quercetin could potentially contribute the highest antioxidative capacity among all flavonoids [69].

Quercetin can reduce intracellular inflammation in multiple ways, including by the inhibition of the NLRP3 inflammasome, the NF-κB pathway, proinflammatory cytokines, and inflammatory mediators [70]. Accordingly, we believe that the CNGsQur, rich in active functional groups derived from quercetin, might also possess a similar anti-inflammatory capability. Thus, we examined the anti-inflammatory ability of the CNGsQur by the NO inhibitory assay (Fig. 3C). Quercetin and CNGsQur270 (100 μg mL−1) both exhibited excellent anti-inflammatory abilities, inhibiting 83.5% and 87.0%, respectively, of NO production induced by lipopolysaccharides (LPS) in RAW 264.7 cells. Our results show that CNGsQur270 display promising antiviral, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. Thus, we next explored their use for in vivo treatment of H1N1 infection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High biocompatibility and SI values of CNGsQur
CNGsQur effectively treat H1N1 infection in the animal model
ce:section.ce:para: The CNGs from quercetin and lysine have been reported for topical bacterial keratitis (BK) treatment [41]. In another study, the CNGs have been used as templates for the growth of photoresponsive copper sulfide (CuS) nanoclusters. The nanocomposite was employed for the eradication of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) associated biofilm on diabetic mice wounds [88]. In the present study, we demonstrated the mild carbonization of quercetin at a specific temperature (270 °C) to form CNGsQur270, which displays multiple antiviral, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. In addition, the CNGsQur270 possesses much superior solubility and biocompatibility compared to quercetin. Furthermore, in H1N1-induced mouse models, we show that the CNGsQur270-treated mice displayed a higher survival rate and potently protective effect against morbidity. In summary, the previous studies reported the CNGs with antibacterial activities to treat keratitis and infected wounds; our present work demonstrated the therapeutic efficacy of multifunctional CNGsQur against H1N1 infection.

In clinical treatment, inhibiting or blocking of the viral replication cycle is not the only key factor that affects antiviral efficacy. It is also indispensable to reduce the cytopathic effects, intracellular ROS levels, and inflammatory factors caused by viral infections [89–92]. Unlike reported antiviral nanoparticles [19–22,65–67], which only focus on removing (blocking) virions, our CNGsQur270 that display multifunctional properties can also alleviate the cytokine storm and eliminate excess ROS caused by the virus. This greatly improves their antiviral clinical effects in the rodent mode. To the best of our knowledge, no effective drugs have been reported that are capable of blocking the entry of the virus in clinical treatment and for the prevention of influenza. We envision the use CNGsQur270 in combination with existing drugs to enhance antiviral efficacy, reduce drug doses, delay the emergence of drug resistance, and have fewer side effects, which will be the focus of our future work.

In this study, quercetin-derived carbonized nanogel (CNGsQur270) that displays multifunctional properties was synthesized by low-temperature (270 °C) pyrolysis. A relatively mild carbonization process promoted the formation of gel-like structures with crosslinked quercetin moieties on the surface and carbonized graphitic structures embedded inside. Our studies revealed that CNGQur270 could suppress reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and inflammation through scavenging free radicals. In vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity assays indicated that CNGsQur270 possesses outstanding biocompatibility. The distinctively pyrolyzed crosslinked polymers of quercetin endow the CNGsQur270 with antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities similar to quercetin; however, exhibiting much better aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and virus inhibitory activity compared to quercetin [60]. The antiviral properties of the CNGsQur270 might be attributed to their high adhesion to the virus envelope, as evidenced by their effective impeding of the adhesion of the H1N1 to the cell membrane of MDCK cells. The CNGsQur270 has a much higher selectivity index (SI > 857.1) relative to antiviral drugs (4–81) in current clinical use [73,74]. Compared to quercetin, aerosol inhalation of CNGsQur270 exhibits a remarkable protective effect against H1N1. Therefore, CNGsQur270 has great potential as a viable alternative for the treatment of H1N1. The mild carbonization process resulting in enhanced water dispersibility and biological activities could be explored for other flavonoids for antibacterial, antifungal, antitumor, and immunomodulatory applications in the future.
